episodeOutline.title: First Bell
episodeOutline.synopsis: On her first day at Ridgemont High, Maya (the player) has ten minutes between the front doors and first bell to figure out who she's going to be here. A hallway tease, a lunchroom full of unspoken rules, and a group project gone sideways force her to choose, again and again, between fitting in, being kind, and being herself — and discover that those aren't always three different things.

characterProfiles.name: Maya
characterProfiles.note: New student navigating her first day; protagonist.

characterProfiles.name: Sam Okafor
characterProfiles.note: Quiet by default, not by nature - a sharp sense of humor he doesn't always spend, and a sketchbook full of comic panels he's slower to share. Given active moments across the episode - the hallway aside, the sketchbook reveal, waving new kids over, and advocating for his own idea before he's cut off - so his fate never depends solely on being rescued.

characterProfiles.name: Jordan Ellis
characterProfiles.note: Confident, magnetic, a little careless with the weight of his own jokes. Not a villain - a mirror, whose warmth toward Maya visibly shifts depending on how she responds to him. Gets one unscripted moment of real self-awareness near the end of the day, separate from anything the player does.

characterProfiles.name: Alex Torres-Mireles
characterProfiles.note: New to Ridgemont like Maya, treats the empty table as an opportunity rather than a consolation prize. Introduces their pronouns plainly, and shares - briefly, without heaviness - what it's like to do that at every new school, so the moment is about Alex's actual experience rather than a checklist.

characterProfiles.name: Devon Park
characterProfiles.note: Competitive and certain, with a specific, human reason underneath it - an older sibling's shadow at the dinner table. Gets one unguarded moment where his certainty slips and the pressure underneath it shows, so his edge reads as personal rather than a flattened stereotype.

characterProfiles.name: Ms. Alvarez
characterProfiles.note: English teacher. Assigns project groups with more intention than she lets on.

scenes.title: New Kid Jitters
scenes.location: Front steps and main hallway of Ridgemont High
scenes.description: Maya stands outside the school clutching a schedule she's already memorized out of nerves. Inside, the hallway is a river of strangers who all seem to know exactly where they belong. She catches her reflection in the trophy case and has a half-second to decide how she wants to walk in.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Anticipatory anxiety - the blank page before the story starts

scenes.title: Hallway Introductions
scenes.description: Maya can't find her locker. Jordan, effortlessly confident and a little sharp-edged, notices her spinning in circles and calls out a joke that lands halfway between funny and mean, loud enough for the hallway to hear. Nearby, a quiet student named Sam flinches - like he's seen this exact game before - then mutters under his breath, just loud enough for Maya to catch: 'Cool locker technique. Very avant-garde.' It's dry, and it's funny, and it's gone as fast as it came.

scenes.title: The Lunchroom Crossroads
scenes.description: Whatever Maya said in the hallway landed - Jordan spots her across the cafeteria and waves her over with real enthusiasm, like she's already passed some invisible test. Across the room, Sam eats alone, headphones on, clearly used to it. An empty table sits by the window, waiting for no one in particular. Maya has a full tray and about four seconds to decide.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The defining crossroads - where do I plant my flag

scenes.title: The Lunchroom Crossroads
scenes.description: Jordan's still friendly when he spots Maya, but there's the faintest edge to it now - like he clocked her redirect in the hallway and isn't sure what to make of a new kid who won't just play along. He waves her over anyway, more out of habit than certainty. Across the room, Sam eats alone, headphones on. An empty table sits by the window, waiting for no one in particular.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The defining crossroads - where do I plant my flag

scenes.title: The Lunchroom Crossroads
scenes.description: Jordan spots Maya and grins like they're already old friends - laughing along in the hallway bought her an easy kind of welcome. He waves her toward his table like it was never in question. Across the room, Sam eats alone, headphones on. An empty table sits by the window, waiting for no one in particular.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The defining crossroads - where do I plant my flag

scenes.title: Sitting With the In-Crowd
scenes.description: The table is fun, fast, and a little cruel. Maya notices, distantly, that she's already talking a little louder than she does at home. Mid-laugh, Sam walks by with a full tray and Jordan sticks out a foot 'as a joke.' The tray goes down. The table erupts. Everyone looks at Maya to see how she'll react.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Complicity test - the cost of belonging

scenes.title: A Quiet Thank You
scenes.description: Jordan's smile tightens for just a second before he covers it with a shrug and moves the table on like nothing happened. For the rest of lunch, though, his jokes have a fraction less warmth aimed her way - nothing dramatic, just a small, real cost. Sam catches Maya's eye and mouths 'thanks' before disappearing toward the trash cans. It's a small thing. It doesn't feel small.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Quiet validation, paid for with a little of Jordan's warmth

scenes.title: The Laugh That Stuck
scenes.description: The laugh is out of Maya's mouth before she decides to let it out. Jordan's arm slides along the back of her chair like a prize just won. Sam doesn't look back on his way to the trash cans, and somehow that's worse than if he had. For the rest of the day, whenever their paths almost cross in the hallway, he finds somewhere else to look.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Belonging bought at a price - the itch of regret

scenes.title: Changing the Subject
scenes.description: Maya jumps in with a quick change of subject - a question about the weekend, a complaint about the cafeteria pizza - anything to move the air in the room. It works, technically. The table's attention slides off the tray on the floor. Sam picks himself up without a word, and nobody quite manages to look at him while he does, Maya included. Nothing blew up. Nothing got fixed either, and the difference sits in her stomach for the rest of the period.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Smoothed over, not resolved - the quiet cost of avoiding the moment

scenes.title: Sitting With Sam
scenes.description: Sam is surprised anyone sat down, then quietly grateful, then - once it's clear Maya isn't leaving - actually funny, in the dry, unhurried way of someone who's been saving up material. He mentions, almost as a throwaway line, that he used to sit with a bigger group before people 'got busy.' It doesn't take a genius to hear what he's not saying. She already knows this is the table she picked, for better or worse.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Unexpected intimacy - real connection in an overlooked corner

scenes.title: His Idea, By the End
scenes.description: Sam gives a short laugh, surprised into it. 'Today, huh?' He doesn't argue - and by the time the bell rings, he's the one nodding toward a table of kids from the art room who might be worth talking to tomorrow. It started as Maya's suggestion. By the end, it's entirely his call.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Encouragement lands, but Sam owns the next step

scenes.title: The Sketchbook
scenes.description: Neither of them says much else. It isn't awkward - it's easy, in the way only two people who've stopped performing for anyone can be. Halfway through lunch, Sam slides a sketchbook across the table without a word: a half-finished comic panel, sharp and funny in a way his quiet voice never hints at. 'You draw?' Maya asks. Sam just shrugs, like it's not a big deal. It clearly is - and clearly his, offered on his own terms.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Real connection, no performance required - and a talent Sam chooses to share

scenes.title: The Door He Opened
scenes.description: Sam hesitates - then, before Maya can say anything else, he's the one waving two kids over from a nearby table, an easy 'hey, sit with us' that seems to surprise even him. Whatever door just opened, he's the one who opened it.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Sam takes the lead - agency, not just gratitude

scenes.title: A Table of Your Own
scenes.description: Another new student drifts over with the specific relief of someone who also didn't know where to sit. 'Hey, I'm Alex - they/them,' they say, already pulling out a chair before Maya can answer. 'Third new school in four years, so I've got the introduction down to a science.' They say it lightly, like a fact rather than a burden, and start building something out of nothing - two strangers deciding, on the spot, what kind of table this is going to be.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Agency in uncertainty - building instead of choosing sides

scenes.title: Building the Table Up
scenes.description: Alex lights up at the idea and is on their feet before Maya finishes the sentence, waving over two kids from the lunch line who look equally unsure where to sit. By the bell, their table of two is a table of five, and Alex looks like they just won something.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Confidence made contagious - belonging as something actively built

scenes.title: Just the Two of Them
scenes.description: Alex seems relieved Maya doesn't push for more, and the conversation settles into something unhurried - favorite shows, worst teachers, the specific dread of a locker combination. By the end of lunch it doesn't feel like a consolation table at all. It just feels like a friendship starting on time.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Depth over volume - a quieter kind of belonging

scenes.title: Caught Looking
scenes.description: Alex catches Maya's eyes drifting toward Jordan's table mid-sentence and just laughs - not unkindly. 'You can want both things, you know. Doesn't make you a fraud.' It's more generous than Maya expected, and it sits with her longer than she lets on.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Honesty about wanting more than one thing - named, not judged

scenes.title: Group Project Roulette
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez reads off project groups for the semester assignment, glancing at her clipboard a beat too long, like she already knows exactly what she's doing. Whatever happened at lunch - the tray, the laugh, the deflection - Sam's name gets called right alongside Maya's, along with a sharp, competitive classmate named Devon Park. Jordan catches Maya's eye across the room for half a second, like he's trying to read which version of today just happened. Sam doesn't look especially thrilled at the pairing. Given the last hour, can she blame him?
scenes.emotionalBeat: The universe insists on a second chance

scenes.title: Group Project Roulette
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez reads the groups, glancing at her clipboard a beat too long, like this pairing isn't as random as it looks. Sam's already grinning slightly when he hears his name paired with Maya's - as if the universe agrees with a decision they'd already made over lunch. Devon Park, sharp and clearly used to being the smartest person in whatever room he's in, rounds out the trio. 'Try to keep up,' he says, half joking. Sam's grin doesn't move an inch.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The universe insists on a second chance

scenes.title: Group Project Roulette
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez reads the groups, and Maya's stomach dips a little when she hears Sam's name next to hers - a face she only knows as 'that quiet kid by the window.' Devon Park is already flipping to a fresh page before his own name finishes being read. Across the room, Alex catches Maya's eye and gives an exaggerated 'good luck' shrug. Whatever table they built at lunch, this is a different kind of group entirely.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The universe insists on a second chance

scenes.title: Whose Idea Wins
scenes.description: Sam finally offers an idea for the project - something visual, a little unconventional, almost like the comic panels he doodles when he thinks no one's looking - and actually pushes back once, quietly but clearly, when Devon talks over him. Devon shuts it down fast and loud anyway, pitching his own plan - the kind of plan that sounds suspiciously rehearsed. For just a second his certainty slips: 'Look, my brother did this exact project three years ago and got an A. I can't - I'm not doing worse than him.' It's gone as fast as it appeared, but Maya catches it. Sam goes quiet in the specific way of someone who's used to being talked over, but not before Maya has the floor for exactly one sentence.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The real test - not the joke this time, but the follow-through

scenes.title: A Good Idea Is a Good Idea
scenes.description: Devon blinks, thrown by the pushback - but the idea's good enough that even he can't argue for long, and the sibling comment hangs there a second longer, unresolved but noted. Sam sits up a little straighter, not just because someone spoke up for him, but because his idea is actually happening now. It was never really about being defended. It was about being heard.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Advocacy as a skill, not just a kindness

scenes.title: Splitting the Difference
scenes.description: The blended pitch takes both ideas and files off the sharpest edges. Devon looks vaguely satisfied at getting some credit; Sam looks quietly relieved his idea survived at all. Nobody's thrilled. Everybody can work with it - which, for a first day, might be its own small win.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Flexibility that still holds its ground

scenes.title: The Idea Nobody Heard
scenes.description: Devon's plan sails through unchallenged. Sam doesn't say another word for the rest of the period. Maya tells herself it wasn't a big deal. The knot in her stomach disagrees - not because Sam needed saving, but because a good idea just got buried and nobody said so.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The cost of silence isn't dramatic - it's just quiet, and it adds up

scenes.title: End of Day
scenes.description: Backpack on, day survived, Maya has a few quiet minutes before the bus. Jordan passes by on his way to a ride and, for half a second, drops the performance: 'Hey - the tray thing, earlier. That was kind of messed up on my part.' It's not quite an apology, but it's not nothing either. Then he's gone, back to being unbothered. Maya's phone buzzes - it could be Sam, it could be Jordan's group chat, it could be nothing at all yet. Tomorrow starts here.

scenes.title: One Real Friend
scenes.description: Maya texts Sam first. The reply comes back fast, with an exclamation point Sam clearly doesn't use often. It's not the whole school. It's one person who's glad she showed up. For a first day, that might be enough - or the start of something bigger.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Grounded belonging - small and true

scenes.title: In With the In-Crowd
scenes.description: Jordan's group chat pings with plans for tomorrow, and Maya's name is already on the list. It feels good - loud, easy, instantly visible. A text from Sam sits underneath it, unopened: 'good luck with the group project tmrw, lol.' She means to answer. She tells herself she'll answer later. Somewhere under the good feeling is the memory of a tray hitting the floor, and Jordan's half-apology that didn't quite finish the sentence.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Belonging with an asterisk - won, but at a cost she'll have to reckon with

scenes.title: Building Something Bigger
scenes.description: Maya texts Sam an idea: what if tomorrow's table had room for more than two people - Alex, maybe even Jordan, if he's willing to lose the audience act. It's a bigger risk than picking a side. It's also the only option where nobody has to eat alone.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Earned hope - belonging as something you build, not something you're granted

choicePoints.prompt: Jordan's joke is still hanging in the air. Everyone's looking at you.
choicePoints.context: Jordan just made a public joke at Maya's expense while she struggled with her locker. Sam flinched nearby and offered a quiet, dry aside, clearly recognizing the pattern.

options.text: Fire back with a confident joke of your own
Notice Sam's flinch and gently redirect the moment - 'Rough first day for everyone, huh?'
Laugh along and play the good sport, matching the hallway's energy

choicePoints.prompt: Tray in hand, where do you sit?
choicePoints.context: Jordan is waving Maya over to the loud table, warmer than usual after the hallway. Sam is alone by the window with headphones on. An empty table sits unclaimed near the windows.

options.text: Sit with Jordan and the crowd
Sit with Sam
Sit alone at the empty table and see what happens

choicePoints.prompt: Tray in hand, where do you sit?
choicePoints.context: Jordan is waving Maya over, a little more guarded than warm. Sam is alone by the window with headphones on. An empty table sits unclaimed near the windows.

options.text: Sit with Jordan and the crowd
Sit with Sam
Sit alone at the empty table and see what happens

choicePoints.prompt: Tray in hand, where do you sit?
choicePoints.context: Jordan is waving Maya over like she's already one of them. Sam is alone by the window with headphones on. An empty table sits unclaimed near the windows.

options.text: Sit with Jordan and the crowd
Sit with Sam
Sit alone at the empty table and see what happens

choicePoints.prompt: Sam's tray is on the floor and the table is laughing. What do you do?
choicePoints.context: Jordan tripped Sam 'as a joke' in front of the whole table. Everyone is watching Maya's reaction.

options.text: Help Sam up and tell Jordan that wasn't cool
Laugh along with the table
Change the subject smoothly and move the conversation on

choicePoints.prompt: Sam just admitted his old friend group 'got busy.' What do you say?
choicePoints.context: Sam has opened up a little about being left behind by his old friends. He seems unsure whether he wants comfort, distraction, or advice.

options.text: Tell him you'd both be better off making new friends - starting today
Just stay with him and let the quiet lunch be enough
Encourage him to wave a couple of other kids over right now

choicePoints.prompt: Alex is talking about starting an after-school club. What's your move?
choicePoints.context: Alex is enthusiastic and a little chaotic, treating the empty table as a blank canvas rather than a punishment.

options.text: Get excited and start inviting other kids over to build a bigger table
Keep it low-key and just enjoy getting to know Alex
Admit, mostly to yourself, that part of you is still curious about Jordan's table

choicePoints.prompt: Devon just shut down Sam's idea before it got a fair hearing. You have the floor.
choicePoints.context: The project group is deciding on a direction. Devon is loud and certain. Sam has gone quiet after trying once to push back. Nobody has actually said whether Sam's idea was good or bad.

options.text: Speak up: 'Wait, I actually think Sam's idea is worth trying.'
Propose a compromise that blends both ideas
Stay quiet and let Devon's plan move forward to avoid a fight

choicePoints.prompt: Your phone is in your hand. What do you do with the last five minutes of the day?
choicePoints.context: The first day is over. Whatever happened at lunch and in the library, Maya gets to decide what tomorrow looks like.

options.text: Text Sam and ask him to sit together again tomorrow
Accept Jordan's invite to sit with the crowd tomorrow
Text Sam a bigger idea: inviting more people, even Jordan, to one table

branches.name: The Authentic Path
branches.description: Maya prioritizes real connection over visibility from the first lunch bell onward, choosing Sam's quiet table, defending his idea under pressure, and reaching out first at day's end.
branches.outcome: Maya ends the day with one genuine friendship and a clear sense of who she wants to be, even if it cost her a seat at the loudest table.

branches.name: The Fit-In Path
branches.description: Maya chases visibility and ease, sitting with Jordan's crowd, laughing along when Sam gets hurt, and accepting an invitation into the in-crowd for tomorrow, regardless of how the project conflict resolves.
branches.outcome: Maya is socially secure but privately unsettled - she got exactly what she reached for and isn't sure it was worth what she gave up, especially with an unanswered text from Sam and Jordan's half-apology still hanging in the air.

branches.name: The Bridge-Builder Path
branches.description: Maya refuses to pick a pre-existing side, building her own table with Alex, defending Sam's idea via compromise, and ending the day proposing to merge the school's separate groups.
branches.outcome: Maya ends the day as the person who makes room for others, having built something new instead of choosing between two existing options.

branches.name: The Costly Courage Path
branches.description: Maya sits with the popular table but breaks from them to defend Sam when it matters, then still drifts back toward Jordan's group the next day regardless of the project outcome.
branches.outcome: A messier, more human outcome - Maya proved she has a spine, but hasn't yet decided which group actually deserves her loyalty.

branches.name: The Smoothed-Over Path
branches.description: Maya keeps the peace at every turn - deflecting the tray incident, staying quiet when Devon steamrolls Sam's idea - prioritizing smoothness over friction at every possible cost point.
branches.outcome: Nothing ever blows up. Nothing ever quite gets resolved either. A quietly unsettling ending that rewards a second playthrough asking 'what if I'd said something?'

emotionalArc: Public vulnerability in the hallway test, softened by a glimpse of Sam's own wit
A lunchroom that already remembers how you handled the hallway - the same crossroads, subtly reweighted
Either quiet validation (at a small cost), itchy regret, or uneasy smoothing-over depending on path - each with an immediate, visible consequence
A second, higher-stakes test of the same instinct during the project conflict, this time framed around whether good ideas get heard, and whether Devon's certainty has a human crack in it
Reflective calm, an unscripted flicker of accountability from Jordan, and a forward-looking choice about identity
Resolution: belonging framed as something built, not simply granted - with real cost attached to the easiest option

characterArcs.arc: Moves from reactive uncertainty ('who do I need to be to survive today') toward intentional identity ('who do I choose to be tomorrow'), tested three times - socially, ethically, and again at day's end - to see if the choice holds under pressure.

Shifts from resigned invisibility to cautious hope (or continued isolation) depending on whether the player notices and acts on his discomfort - but is also given his own moments of wit, creative pride, and initiative (the hallway aside, the sketchbook, waving new kids over, pushing back on Devon once unprompted) that exist independent of the player's choices.

Mostly a mirror, reflecting back whatever energy the player brings - visibly warmer or cooler toward Maya depending on her hallway response - but given one unscripted moment of genuine, unresolved self-awareness at the bus loop, making clear that accountability is something he's starting to reckon with on his own terms, not just something Maya's kindness fixes for him.

Represents the low-risk, high-reward option of building something new rather than choosing between existing groups. Introduces their pronouns and briefly, plainly, what it's like to do that at every new school - identity as lived experience, not just a label - and is present only on the path where the player takes that risk.

Stays competitive and certain throughout, but is given a single unguarded moment - a sibling's shadow slipping out mid-argument - that reframes his edge as personal insecurity rather than a flattened stereotype of achievement.

traitMapping.description: Rewarded when the player takes social risks with visible stakes: standing up to Jordan, sitting alone, speaking up for Sam's idea, or initiating contact rather than waiting to be included.

Rewarded when the player notices and responds to Sam's discomfort even when it costs social standing, and when they advocate for someone being overlooked. Slightly penalized when the player smooths over harm instead of naming it.

Rewarded when the player reads a room and adjusts approach without abandoning their values - deflecting conflict smoothly, proposing compromises, or building new social structures instead of picking an existing side.

relationshipDynamics.dynamic: The emotional core of the episode - a relationship that can deepen into real friendship, curdle into guilt, or never quite form, depending entirely on several separate moments of attention, with Sam given real agency at each of them.

A test of social gravity - Jordan represents the pull of easy belonging, and his visible warmth toward Maya rises and falls with how much she plays along, independent of her feelings toward Sam. His late, unscripted flicker of self-awareness keeps him human without letting him off the hook.

A lower-stakes practice run for standing up for someone else, mirroring the earlier tray incident but in an academic setting where the player has more clear standing to intervene, and where Devon's own pressures are briefly visible.
An optional, lighter relationship representing the path of building something new rather than joining or rejecting existing groups, grounded in Alex's own lived experience of starting over.

replayHooks: The hallway response now visibly changes Jordan's warmth in the very next scene, so a second playthrough can test all three openings against the same crossroads.
Three genuinely different lunchroom scenes (popular table, Sam's table, empty table) mean a first playthrough only sees a third of the content.
The tray incident has three distinct, visible aftermaths (defend, join, deflect), each with its own real cost, inviting players to compare all three.
The empty-table path now branches three ways of its own (recruit, bond, admit curiosity about Jordan), each coloring the rest of the day differently.

The project-conflict scene plays very differently emotionally depending on whether the player has already bonded with or hurt Sam, and Devon's one unguarded moment lands differently depending on what's come before.
Three distinct endings (quiet friendship, secure popularity, bridge-building) each feel emotionally complete but tonally opposite, encouraging comparison.
The 'Costly Courage' and 'Smoothed-Over' branches produce messier, more ambiguous outcomes that curious players will want to find on a second run.
Jordan's late, unscripted moment of accountability at the bus loop lands differently depending on what the player already knows about him - worth noticing on a replay.
educationalGoals: Model that confidence can look like standing alone, speaking up, or simply owning a decision - not just being the loudest person in the room
Show empathy as an active choice made under social cost, not just a passive feeling, with visible consequences either way
Demonstrate adaptability as flexibility in approach without loss of core values
Illustrate that first impressions are made of many small choices, not one big one, and that those choices carry forward
Present belonging as something buildable rather than something granted by the 'right' group
Frame speaking up for someone's idea as advocacy for good ideas, not just kindness toward a person - a skill worth developing on its own terms
conversationStarters: If you had to sit somewhere on your first day, which table do you think you'd actually choose - not which one you'd want to choose?
Was there a moment in the story where staying quiet felt like the safe choice? Was it?
Do you think Jordan is a bad person, or just someone who's never had to think about how his jokes land? Does his moment at the bus loop change your answer?
Which ending felt the most 'earned' to you, and which felt like it cost the most?
Have you ever had a moment like the spilled tray - where you had half a second to decide who you were going to be?
Sam draws, jokes, and eventually leads in his own quiet way - what did you notice about him that had nothing to do with how other people treated him?
Devon has a moment where his certainty slips - what do you think is really driving him?

designNotes: Key changes: (1) Split the single lunchroom scene into three variants (confident/redirect/easygoing) so the hallway choice now has a visible, mechanical consequence on Jordan's warmth, addressing the 'no consequence' critique on choice-1. (2) Split choice-3c into three distinct aftermath scenes (recruit/bond/curious) instead of converging on one scene, fixing the QA blocker about a meaningless choice; added a line where Alex names the 'wanting both things' tension explicitly. (3) Added traitMapping to choice-1, choice-3b, and choice-3c, all previously missing. (4) Updated all five branch triggeredBy lists to include every choice point on the path, using a ':*' wildcard notation where the option doesn't affect the outcome, per QA's completeness request. (5) Strengthened Sam's agency: he now pushes back once on Devon unprompted in scene-6, in addition to his existing hallway aside, sketchbook reveal, and table-invite moments. (6) Added a real, felt cost to the 'defend' choice (Jordan's warmth cools slightly) and made Sam's avoidance explicit in the 'join' aftermath, per the ethics reviewer's note that the ethical choice shouldn't read as costless. (7) Gave Devon one unguarded, humanizing line about his brother's shadow, breaking up the 'model minority' reading without erasing his competitiveness. (8) Gave Alex a one-line reflection on repeatedly introducing their pronouns at new schools, so the identity marker is tied to lived experience rather than a checklist. (9) Strengthened the 'fit-in' ending with an unanswered Sam text, making the emotional cost of that path more prominent per the ethics reviewer's request, without punishing the player narratively for choosing it. I did not structurally consolidate the three lunch-table branches into one scene as the game designer alternatively suggested, since that would remove the meaningful divergence the replay hooks depend on - instead I leaned further into replayability as the intentional resolution to that tension.
uncertainties: The three lunchroom-variant scenes (confident/redirect/easygoing) add scene count without changing the choice-2 destination logic - this satisfies the 'visible consequence' request cheaply, but a richer fix might have Jordan's altered warmth also change dialogue inside scene-4a-popular-table itself; I kept that scene singular to avoid combinatorial explosion (3 hallway paths x 3 lunch paths x 3 tray reactions). Worth discussing whether that's an acceptable simplification.

I used a ':*' wildcard notation in branch triggeredBy lists, which isn't formally specified in the schema - if the downstream system requires literal enumerated option ids, these five branches would need to be expanded into many more concrete branch objects (e.g., branch-fit-in-first would multiply by 3 for choice-4 and 3 for choice-1, or by the lunchroom-variant ids). Flagging this as a possible follow-up if branches need to be machine-parsed rather than documentation-only.

I did not restructure the 66%-unseen-content concern (game designer's major note) since doing so would undercut the replay-hook design intentionally built into this episode; I've reframed it in design notes as an accepted tradeoff rather than a defect, but a producer might reasonably disagree given production cost per scene.

Devon and Alex's added humanizing lines are single sentences by design, to keep the 10-15 minute runtime intact - if the ethics reviewer wants deeper characterization, that likely requires a longer episode or a dedicated future episode centered on one of them.
